original_abstract: Research interest in machine learning (ML) has surged over the past few decades, yet for many researchers without a deep background in statistics, evaluating and comparing model performance remains a challenge. This paper providing a comprehensive introduction to the most common evaluation metrics used across typical supervised ML tasks, including binary, multi-class, and multi-label classification, regression, image segmentation, object detection, and information retrieval. We provide a roadmap for selecting appropriate statistical tests, acquiring sufficient metric values for robust testing, and correctly interpreting the results. Furthermore, we demonstrate these concepts through practical case studies, such as comparing convolutional neural networks designed to classify infectious diseases from X-rays and detect tumors in positron emission tomography (PET) imaging. Our goal is to bridge the gap between ML development and rigorous statistical validation.
humanized_abstract: In recent years, machine learning (ML) research has garnered significant attention in the literature, particularly from those without formal statistical training. Understanding how to evaluate the performance of a given model is rarely taken as a first steps in developing a novel algorithm. This manuscript gives a comprehensive overview of metrics pertinent to some of the more common supervised learning problems: binary/multi-class classification, multi-label classification, regression, image segmentation, object detection, and information retrieval. Additionally, the manuscript outlines a methodology behind using statistical tests, describes how to obtain relevant metric values for said testing, and provides interpretations for various analyses, while including corresponding case studies to verify their applicability, using for example, the performance between models with different architectures designed to classify diseases using X-ray images, or to detect tumors in PET (positron emission tomography) images.
